title: Psalms 53
subtitle: The Fool Says There Is No God (II)

heb.paragraph: "The fool (nābāl) says in his heart, “There is no God”" (v.1). Nearly identical to Psalm 14. Both preserved in the Psalter — Psalm 14 uses YHWH; Psalm 53 uses Elohim (part of the Elohistic Psalter). God surveys humanity; all have turned aside; no one does good.

notes.note: "The fool says there is no God." The universal corruption diagnosis repeated from Psalm 14. God looks down; all are corrupt. "Not even one."

sections.header: Verses 4–6 — “Oh, That Salvation Would Come from Zion!”

heb.paragraph: "Oh, that salvation (yəshūʿâ) for Israel would come out of Zion!" (v.6). The same cry as Psalm 14:7. The evildoers are struck with dread where there was nothing to dread (v.5) — the key difference from Psalm 14. God scatters the bones of attackers. The messianic longing returns: salvation from Zion.

notes.note: "God scattered the bones of those who attacked you." The Elohistic version adds this detail: the enemy’s defeat is more vivid.
"Oh, that salvation would come from Zion!" Repeated from Psalm 14. The longing for the Messiah persists through the Psalter.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: The one who looks down from heaven to find understanding (vv.2-3)
ppl.text: This is a near-duplicate of Psalm 14 with one significant change: Elohim replaces Yahweh throughout, and verse 5 diverges dramatically. Where Psalm 14:5-6 mentions the righteous as refuge, Psalm 53:5 describes God scattering the bones of besieging enemies — suggesting a specific military deliverance. The Elohistic revision may reflect liturgical use in northern Israel or a theological emphasis on God's universal sovereignty over his covenant-specific name.

ppl.name: The Fool (nabal)
ppl.role: Practical atheist whose denial corrupts behavior (v.1)
ppl.text: As in Psalm 14, the nabal denies God 'in his heart' — a volitional moral rejection, not a philosophical position. The corruption flows from belief to behavior: 'they are corrupt, and their ways are vile' (v.1). The Hebrew connects nabal to withering and decay (cf. Nabal in 1 Sam 25:25, whose wife says 'he is just like his name — his name means Fool'). The dual placement of this psalm (14 and 53) in the Psalter underscores its importance as a diagnosis of the human condition.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Attributed author voicing the Mahalath tune (superscription)
ppl.text: The unique superscription adds 'according to Mahalath' — possibly a melancholy tune (related to chalah, 'to be sick/weak') or a musical instrument. David's longing for salvation 'out of Zion' (v.6) makes God's dwelling place the source of deliverance, connecting the psalm's universal diagnosis of human sin to Israel's particular hope in God's presence at the temple.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVOh, that salvation for Israel would come out of Zion! When God restores his people, let Jacob rejoice and Israel be glad!ESVOh, that salvation for Israel would come out of Zion! When God restores the fortunes of his people, let Jacob rejoice, let Israel be glad.NIVGod looks down from heaven on all mankind to see if there are any who understand, any who seek God.ESVGod looks down from heaven on the children of man to see if there are any who understand, who seek after God.

rec.text: ""The fool says there is no God." The universal corruption diagnosis repeated from Psalm 14. God looks down; all are corrupt. "Not even one.""

rows.label: Zion’s Hope — 53:4–6
rows.text: Bones scattered. "Oh, that salvation would come from Zion!" Messianic longing.

hebtext.note: "The fool (nābāl) says in his heart, “There is no God”" (v.1). Nearly identical to Psalm 14. Both preserved in the Psalter — Psalm 14 uses YHWH; Psalm 53 uses Elohim (part of the Elohistic Psalter). God surveys humanity; all have turned aside; no one does good.

"Oh, that salvation (yəshūʿâ) for Israel would come out of Zion!" (v.6). The same cry as Psalm 14:7. The evildoers are struck with dread where there was nothing to dread (v.5) — the key difference from Psalm 14. God scatters the bones of attackers. The messianic longing returns: salvation from Zion.

themes.note: Judgment: universal corruption. Prophecy: salvation from Zion. The Elohistic twin of Psalm 14.
